sentences.text: Yeah, I stopped my cousin from jumping out, or he would be killed as well, and didn't make sense.
Soon, as the dead were collected and taken away we were-- a few airmen came from the airbase with an officer.
And we were marched to the airbase, to the barracks.

While this was going on in the ghetto-- you know, the collection of the people, the selection-- another group of Gestapo went to the airbase-- there were over 300 Jews working there, with their families at the airbase-- and cleaned it out there.
Took all their wives.
They took their wives, their children away, and many of the older ones and left only about 100, 105 people out of the 300.
And they attached those people to the transport that went to the bigger ghetto-- I told you-- in Reichshof, that my little brother, and my cousin, and his boy Michael went to.
And we were marched over there.
It was snowing.
It was a Friday morning, the 4th of December.
It was snowing.
And while we were marching there, a few Jews that were hidden-- you know, on the road somewheres-- they saw us march to the airbase.
They jumped out and attached themselves.
And we were afraid that we get to the airbase, and they will count us and there's going to be more, you know, et cetera.
But the-- the airmen didn't give a damn.
I mean, they couldn't care less.
Their job was to bring us to the airbase.
So there we were set up in the barracks there.
I have to tell you an incident when I got there.
There was a Jewish fellow used to be a neighbor of ours.
And he was a Hasid.
You know what a Hasid is?
A very religious man.
He had a bunch of children.
And he was a [NON-ENGLISH] to the [INAUDIBLE] of a rabbi-- a famous Hasidic rabbi.
And he used to be a neighbor of ours.
And when we got there, you know, and his wife had been taken away.
All his children had been taken [INAUDIBLE] with them.
He started lighting the candles for the Shabbos, and he started praying.
And I became very mad at him because I couldn't see this man-- his wife has been taken away.
His children has been taken away.
And I accosted him and I told him, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
How can you possibly be praying?
Who are you praying to?
They just took your family away.
You know.
You're probably never going to see them again.
You know.
But he was praying.
Anyway, there we were, about 120 people.
And I'm going to make it brief, because 1943 there was rather uneventful.
As I told you before, we-- when we worked for the military-- this time we worked for the German Luftwaffe-- for the air force-- we did not experience anything whatever from them that was adverse.
I never heard an anti-Semitic word.
And I-- we worked with our armbands amongst them.
And we used to get food.
For example, my father worked there-- not as a glazier, but in the kitchen-- and he used to bring home food from the kitchen.
A girl-- a friend of mine, Sonia-- she lives now in Australia.
She survived at Schindler's also with me-- she worked in the officer's club.
And she always brought food from the officer's club.

And when I visited in Australia a few years ago, she was telling me an incident where she and another girl-- a Polish girl who worked with her-- were called in by the officer in charge of the officer's club.
And he screamed and yelled at them that they supposedly were stealing food and taking it with them to the barracks.
And another soldier stood there.
He apparently was the accuser, that he caught them doing it.
And she said the following-- and he said if he catches them again, he's going to deliver them to the Gestapo.
The following day that same officer calls them in again.
And he says, look, what I told you yesterday, forget.
Take as much food as you want.
So she was telling me that.
It is just to give you an idea that, you know, it depends what you were exposed to, you know.
If you speak, most Jews were exposed to the SS all the time, you see.
Whereas I was also exposed to the Wehrmacht and to the Luftwaffe where you saw decent people, you see?
So, anyway, we were there in 1943.
Around February of 1943, you may remember, the Germans suffered their first defeat at Stalingrad.
That was a bad time.
And there was the uprising starting in-- in Warsaw, you see.
And my brother was still in that town, Jaslo, cleaning up from the ghetto there.
And he was glazing there.
He worked there as a glazier.
He was also glazing in jail.

That's how we found out that two girls from my hometown tried to escape from a little camp in Trzebinia-- which was a village between our town and Jaslo-- with two Ukrainian guardsmen who fell in love with them.
And they decided that they are going to run away and they're going to go to Hungary, because the Hungarian border wasn't far from there.
You see?
So-- but they were caught.
So the two girls were jailed in Jaslo.
And my cousin-- my-- my brother-- used to go to the jail.
And he knew the jailer very well.
And he took out a note from them to send to us.
We had a deal with this Polish guy, [?
Socha ?], that he would-- whoever's alive is going to send him a letter, and he will deliver it to us at the airbase, because he used to, then, transport stuff for the air base, for the Luftwaffe.
So we used to get communication continually from him.
All of a sudden, we hear nothing.
And we knew something is bad.

It was at that time that the airbase was petitioned to let my father-- to let my father, me, and my cousin come to glaze the [SPEAKING GERMAN]-- that is this Institute for Race-- for Race Research, in [?
Krossen ?], now a town.
The head of it was an obersturmfuhrer-- a-- a SS man.
So as we went there we had to go pick up glass in Jaslo.
So I asked the obersturmfuhrer whether he could be kind enough to find out where my brother is.
You know, that my brother is in Jaslo.
Could I go with him to pick up the glass-- me and my cousin-- and see my brother?
So he said yes, and we went with him.
I come to Jaslo, and there are the Jews there.
You know.
And I talk to them and they say nothing.
They say nothing.
There was a girl by the name of Frida who still was alive-- she also survived at Schindler's-- from around my hometown-- we were  four of us from around my hometown.
We survived at Schindler's-- and they wouldn't say a word.
You know.
The Gestapo man-- the SS man, obersturmfuhrer comes back.
And I said, where-- where's my brother?
Do you know where my brother is?
He said he inquired.
He isn't there anymore.
Period.
And we didn't know what happened.
I didn't find out until long after the war, when I accosted that Frida to tell me what happened.
And she described exactly what happened.
They caught him throw a letter into the mailbox to this fellow [?
Socha, ?] you know, to bring it to us.
And they caught him.
The following day, this Hauch, the Gestapo man-- the short one-- she described him to me and I-- I knew them all by name-- called the Jews together.
That-- there were about 20 of them there.
They made a circle.
And he called out my brother.
And he took out his gun and shot him.
Not a word from him.
Not one single word from him.
Later he gave a speech to the others.
This is what is going to happen if any of you are going to do anything that's illegal.
So now my brother was gone.
So, again, we were at the airbase, 1943, nothing remarkable happened there.
We were fed reasonably well, although we had to use SS foods.
We used to throw it away.
We used to pick it up at the camp-- concentration camp Trzebinia near our town, where there were over 1,000 Jews there.
And we had to pick up the provisions there.
And this Oberzahlmeister Frankfurter used to take some of the guys from the-- from our barracks to pick up the food.
He never believed that the Germans were capable of doing what we were telling him.
When he went there he said, now he believes it.
What he saw-- what went on in that camp, you see, in Trzebinia, he believed what we were telling him.
You see?
Anyway, he was funny, because he would-- for example, we march to work.
He'd want us to march like soldiers, he said, so that when you go to Palestine after the war that you know how to march properly, et cetera.
He was a real nice man, Oberzahlmeister Frankfurter.
His boss was Hauptzahlmeister Flahman.
There was a-- I have to tell you that story-- there was a captain in the-- at the airbase.
His name was Captain [?
Hoelzl. ?] [?
Haupt ?] [? von ?] [?
Hoelzl. ?] He used to come to the barracks.
He loved to talk to my father, politics.
And I remember he would come.
When Germany was bombed by the Allies, he would tell my father, you know, that Hamburg was bombed, Frankfurt was bombed.
So many people were killed.
And he told my father, you know why this is happening to us?
Because what we do to you.
I mean, a most wonderful man.
After the war, you know, if I had time, I'd want to go and find him.
I knew he was from Frankfurt.
There were some good men.
There was Major Hildebrand, a very nice, fine man.
And the colonel in charge, Obers Guigold in charge, was also a very fine individual.
And Mr.
Langsome, who was the head of the Jews-- a Jewish, also, fellow-- who used to communicate with them.
You know.
Used to tell us how fine they were.
You see.
Anyway, at the end of 1943 the order came that the entire command of the Luftwaffe in our town-- you see, this was a [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That was-- it was a school for-- a technical school for flyers.
No, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Four.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
It was under the command of Breslau.
Anyway, the entire command-- the whole [GERMAN]-- is to go to the east.
Because now they were having to fight in the east.
And, of course, they had Stalingrad.
You know.
Now they had the big battles at Kursk, you know, at Kirvoy Rog.
And they had to go to the east.
Of course, they're all young kids at the airbase.
And a command from the east-- tired soldiers are going to come and take over the airbase.
Now we got scared.
What's going to happen to us?
So Mr.
Langsome went to talk to the-- to the people in charge at the airbase.
And they promised that they will do their best to keep us.
They will talk to the command that's going to take over.
You know, introduce us to them and ask them that they should try to keep us.
Because the SS always wanted us back.
You see?
Now, to give you the extent of their cooperation with us, that when the SS came from Krakow-Plaszow to visit-- to see what goes on-- they had the order to hold them at the gate.
So they let us know that they're there, and we took the pictures up from our barracks and threw everything aside so that they won't see it.
Because one time-- the first time they came-- was-- was a Untersturmfuhrer [?
Jan ?] from Krakow-Plaszow.
He came to inspect.
And he went through the barracks, threw the pictures down-- you know, in our rooms, et cetera-- and he screamed and yelped, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means, the Jews live here like God in France.
You know.
And he threw everything.
From that time on they held him there.
And they let us know that they are coming.
And we cleaned up and we took everyone out of the barracks.
Whoever wasn't sick, sick, et cetera, so that the barracks were empty.
And, anyway, so the end of '43 new command comes along.
They introduce to us new command.
They promise they will do their best.
But they brought along with them over 400 prisoners of war from the-- from Central Asia.
Uzbekis.
And those Uzbekis, they didn't like them.
They real-- they wanted us.
But the SS said, look, you've got 400 people.
You don't need the hundred some Jews.
You've got to give them back.
And they took us away.
And that was the end of '43, early '44.
And we were taken away and transported to Trzebinia, which was a camp that had over 1,000 people, including my cousin and that Frida that [INAUDIBLE] was there.
You know.
That was black Joe, the-- the blacksmith who survived Auschwitz who just died a year ago in New Jersey.
And they-- they had taken all of them to Auschwitz, you see.
And there was only a small group left, including that Frida.
You see.
And we were then sent there.
And we were cleaning up the barracks there for a few days.
And then we were transported to Krakow-Plaszow.
You know.
In January.
We would come-- you know, in cattle transports.
You know.
But not like the others, you know, packed in over 100.
Because we weren't many, you see.
And we arrived there.
Now, my cousin, the tool and die maker with his little boy, and my little brother, were taken when the ghetto in Reichshof was liquidated in 1943.
And they perished in Auschwitz.
So they died in Auschwitz.
So now it was just myself, my father, and my cousin.
We were in Krakow-Plaszow, and we were assigned to the glaziers there.
I have to tell you an incident when I arrived there.
If you had seen in those heads of the Jews that they shot, you know, and laid them out, and we had to march by-- one amongst them was by the name of Manek Furber.
I remember when we arrived there he gave us a little talk.
He says, this is not the Luftwaffe anymore, fellas.
Here's a completely different story.
You have to work this.
And I signed us to shovel mud, because it was very muddy there, and so on.
I walked over to him, and I asked him whether he could assign my father to the shoemakers, because the head of the shoemaker department was a man from my hometown who lived in Krakow.
You know.
He took his hand and gave me a hit on my face.
How you dare come to talk to me like this?
Now, when you read some of the books, and some of the relatives of this Furber will tell you what a wonderful guy he was, Manek Furber.
I can only tell you that I don't know many that shed one tear for any of those guys that they shot over there.
They were horrible.
I just have to tell you that incident.
It was horrible in that Krakow-Plaszow.
We weren't used to this, you see, because we had the Garden of Eden, you know, at the airbase, you know, relatively speaking.
So there we were.
I have to tell you one incident that happened.
We were glazing in a barrack, and across the road was another barrack where there were Ukrainian guardsmen.
And they were tending to the horses there.
So one showed me a loaf of bread he wants to sell me, through the window.
There was lots of black-marketeering going on.
So as he shows it to me I go over there to pick up the bread and talk to him.
As I am in there, I see a Gestapo man-- a SS man.
You know, not a Ukrainian-- a German SS man.
We used to call him [NON-ENGLISH], the bear, because he looked like a bear.
Him coming towards the barracks.
He saw me in there.
And as the Ukrainian guardsman sees him he tells me, get right under my bunk.
For me to hide under his bunk.
He runs up the ladder where he had hay, or straw, and started feigning like he was working there, you know.
The SS man comes in-- [NON-ENGLISH], the bear comes in.
He takes one peek and he sees me under there.
He takes out his revolver, you know, and he tells me, stick your head out.
I thought, well, that's the end of me.
I may just well stick my head out.
So, you know, I don't want to suffer.
So I stuck my head out, and he takes the gun back, puts it back-- his revolver-- and he says, I'm not going to shoot you.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], he says.
I'm not going to waste a bullet on you.
And he has me come out there.
Now he has me undress naked.
I undress naked.
He takes my uniform and he starts feeling up to see whether I don't have something hidden in there, you know.
And he feels something.
And he takes his bayonet, opens it, and there's a $5 bill and two singles that I had sewn in into the collar.
Now he says, aha.
Now he takes me, and he starts whipping me with his whips, because he used to walk with these big whips.
And I run like a poisoned rat from one corner to the next.
And he after me and after me.
He whips me back and forth.
I was screaming.
And he keeps whipping me.
Finally, he was huffing and puffing.
He was-- he got tired.
He couldn't breathe.
So he stopped.
Now he takes out his little book, no?
Writes down my number.
And he said, tomorrow, on the appellplatz, which is the place where we used to be counted, that god forbid no one is missing-- you are going to hang.
There was hanging going on every so often if they caught someone steal something, or for whatever reason.
They were hanging people.
You are going to hang tomorrow.
Well, there's nothing I can do.
He lets me go.
I go back to the barrack.
My father watches through the window.
My father had a bottle of whiskey.
He gives it to me.
And I said, where did you get the whiskey?
He says, the-- the SS man, you know-- and I'll never forget him because he looked like Mikoyan, you know, the Soviet fellow that used to walk with Krushchev around-- he looked just like him.
He was a higher rank.
He was a scharfuhrer.
He said, I saw what happened to your son.
Please give him this.
And we are not all like him.
Isn't it amazing?
He is an SS man [INAUDIBLE] gives me a bottle of whiskey.
I still see that guy.
I wish I knew his name, you see.
I had to tell you that incident.
But, anyway, during the-- during the selection of people to Auschwitz, you know, in Krakow, my father, my cousin were selected to go Auschwitz, to the gas chambers.
My father was only 50-couple years old.
My cousin was only his 30s, but he had a back problem so he walked a little bit crooked.
My father was very skinny, but he was always skinny, like this.
And he was selected to go there.
Now, his latest words to me was, as he walked away from me is-- there was only one thing I wish-- I tried to give him my bread.
I had a piece of bread to give to him.
He says, I won't need it.
He gives it back to me.
He didn't want to take it.
So he said to me, there's one thing [INAUDIBLE] I lost everybody.
And I don't care whether I live or don't live.
If I live another 10, 20 years.
So I will eat another ton of potatoes.
These are the words to me-- another few tons of potatoes.
I lost everybody.
You are the only one that's left.
There's only one wish I have-- be a mensch.
That's it.
And he walked away with my cousin.
Contrary to what you saw in Schindler's movie-- Schindler's movie is incorrect in this regard.
In Schindler's movie, they were shoved off to this side, to this side, and sent straight to the station.
That was not.
The week before, they took their names off, and was the following week that they were called out and sent to the station and sent away.
So this-- this way I lost-- I found out that they were gassed because there were two brothers who survived the war.
And they were in Auschwitz.
And they used to work near the gas chambers.
And they saw my father and cousin.
And one day a fellow at the camp comes-- he's looking for me.

And he said he had-- there was a little note amongst the boards, you know, that they shipped from Auschwitz to our camp-- there was a note that they saw my father and my-- my cousin go to the gas chambers.
So now I was left alone.
There I was.
And then in October, you know, as they were withdrawing, you know, we had to clean up the mass graves that were there at the camp.
And I was assigned to work in these mass graves that you saw in the movie.
And I was in there the mass grave.
And I remember I worked with a shovel.
And there was a redhead man with a baby next to him.
And I'm not wanting to hurt the body with a shovel.
Was bending down and tried to gently lift him off.
And one of the kapos didn't like the way I worked.
Now, all of a sudden I saw all stars in my eyes, and there was all kinds of-- of stuff from the dead body in my mouth and my eyes, and blood was running down my nose-- because I was working too slow.
That was an incident that happened in that place.
And then, thereafter-- this was just before we were shipped off to Schindler's-- and then I was all of a sudden assigned to go to Schindler's, to Sudetenland.
In Brunnlitz [?
Brissau ?], which was Sudetenland.
And then we were shipped off to Gross-Rosen, which was a camp in Germany.
There we stayed a couple days.
A horrible camp.
We arrived there, and the afternoon and evening we stood out.
It was cold.
Was the 15th of October.
Was very cold and was drizzling a little bit.
I stood in the middle so the other guys kept me warm.
The other guys were freezing.
And then they took us in to disinfect us.
And they would smear us here, and here, and here.
We moved on, then they cut our hair.
And the guy who cut the hair-- I thought was a Ukrainian.
That's the way he looked to me.
He was sitting in this chair and didn't say a word.
He was a prisoner, a Ukrainian prisoner.
Not one word.
But he took a hit over the head for you to kneel down.
And he cut a swath alongside, a vertical swath so you are recognized.
Shaved it off, you know, and rest of the hair was cut short, you know.
And so then they shoved us in to take showers.
And then they put us in a room.
We were sitting on our rears, and we had to spread our knees.
And the next guy into you, and the next guy into you.
And that guy stood there with a whip to squeeze in, squeeze in.
I thought I cannot survive this.
I mean, my knees were hurting.
My hips were hurting.
I could not-- it was awful.
There was no air.
Finally, someone opened a window.
And we sat there like this almost all night.

And then early in the morning they had-- they took us to a room where they had a bunch of shoes, a bunch of shirts, and we had to run very fast to grab shoes and to grab shirts, because we were naked sitting there.
Grab shoes, grab shirts.
Because the uniforms we had from Krakow were taken away.
And when you grabbed [INAUDIBLE] two left shoes, right shoes, [? oh, ?] you were hit.
I grabbed a nightgown.
Must have been from a victim, a nightgown.
And it had an embroidery [INAUDIBLE].
It was very long.
It came in handy, because I developed massive diarrhea, massive.
And there was no paper.
I have never seen a camp as clean in my life.
There wasn't a shred of paper anywheres.
So I kept tearing off little pieces from that nightgown and used it.
My shirt then came to here.
I kept using that up.
But this was Gross-Rosen.
Fortunately, after a few days, we were taken away and shipped to Schindler's.
And we arrived there at Schindler's, which was a God-given when we saw him.
We saw messiah.
Soon the women came, you know.
And I was at Schindler's from that time when I was assigned to the glaziers until liberation, May 8.
Now, at Schindler's, being at the glaziers, initially our rations were very small.
We had one eighth of a loaf of bread of about this size.
If you ever buy Lithuanian bread here in Chicago, that about more or less the size of the loaf.
One eighth of that.
I thought that that was for breakfast.
I ate it the first day, you know.
Then I found out, no, that's for the whole day.
So the next day I was just biting off tiny little crumbs and kept it in my pocket.
The soup was beet.
Beets.
Beet soup.
Water.
And it depended what you got dished, because the guys who were dishing out the soup had their favorites.
They got the bottom.
I got the water.
I peed maroon for weeks thereafter, because that's all I got, you see, and some ersatz coffee.
